<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: labor-os-and-typed-jobs
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/labor-os-and-typed-jobs.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/labor-os-and-typed-jobs.qmd@5b2400ea5
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Labor OS and Typed Jobs

The stack cannot let intelligence turn directly into action.

Planning can decide what should happen. Verification can decide what seems
supported. Review can decide what may proceed. But execution needs a smaller
unit than "the agent did it." It needs a work record: a typed job with a
parent contract, lifecycle state, permissions, approvals, expected artifact,
failure behavior, audit trail, and replay boundary.

That is the purpose of a Labor OS. It is not a personality layer for agents.
It is the operating surface where accepted work becomes bounded labor.

Once execution is organized around typed jobs, action becomes inspectable.
The system can ask whether a job is drafted, locked, awaiting approval,
approved, running, delivered, evidence-ready, quarantined, replayed, or
retired. It can distinguish an output that exists from an output that is
authorized, verified, replayable, and safe to reuse. It can stop a high-impact
job at the approval gate instead of relying on a model's prediction that
approval would probably be granted.

The point is not to make AI labor slower. The point is to make it governable.

## Problem

Advanced AI systems need execution without ambient authority.

A plan node is not yet work. A chat response is not a work order. A generated
file is not necessarily authorized output. If the system lets a model slide
from suggestion to tool use to side effect without a work record, the stack
loses the ability to inspect what happened.

The Labor OS chapter names the execution boundary. Intelligence must become
typed work with lifecycle, permissions, artifacts, logs, and approvals. Each
dispatchable unit should say what it is, where it came from, what it may touch,
who or what approved it, which runtime adapter it will use, which artifact it
must return, and how failure will be handled.

Without that boundary, the project cannot make the later evidence claims it
wants to make. Artifact graphs need job parentage. Claim ledgers need to know
whether an output was verified or merely delivered. Runtime adapters need
permission envelopes. Human approval needs a state that can actually block
dispatch. Recursive improvement needs a way to separate proposed work from
accepted effects.

Typed jobs are the hinge between thinking and doing.

## Why Agents Are Not Enough

The word agent hides too much.

An agent may plan, call tools, ask a human, edit a file, summarize evidence,
or start another process. Those may be useful behaviors, but the word does not
by itself say which action was authorized, which permission was scoped, which
approval was required, which artifact was expected, which checks ran, or which
residual remained.

AutoGen is useful external vocabulary for multi-agent conversation patterns:
agents can converse, use tools, include humans, and coordinate workflows.
SWE-bench is useful external vocabulary for real software tasks with repository
context, patches, execution environments, and tests. Those sources help orient
the Labor OS claim, but this repository has not run AutoGen, reproduced
SWE-bench, or shown that Talos-style typed jobs solve either system's tasks.

The ASI Stack asks a different systems question: what would have to wrap
agentic work before it could count as governed execution?

The answer is a job record. A governed system should care less about whether
the worker is one model, several agents, a tool chain, a human, or a hybrid
process. It should care that the work enters through a typed packet and exits
with a receipt.

## Core Claim

The execution layer should convert plans into typed jobs managed by a
governed labor operating system. In the live book, this claim remains a design
rationale at `argument` support.

Talos supplies the main labor-OS frame: contract locks, typed job lifecycles,
deterministic control planes, evidence-bound artifacts, audit, replay,
isolation, adjudication, and delivery. VIEA connects that execution surface to
intent, command contracts, artifact graphs, permission envelopes, runtime
adapters, approval roles, side-effect classes, feedback, and residuals.
GenesisCode sharpens the separation between proposals and effects through
capability boundaries, deterministic effect logs, obligations, provenance, and
semantic patches. Software Magic Grimoire contributes workflow structure:
roles, objectives, context, constraints, procedures, output contracts,
verification, failure behavior, entry and exit rules, loops, and recovery
paths. The Talos Markdown release and MoECOT record remain
connector/source-note context, not locally reproduced runtime evidence.

The current repository has record-level and finite-proof support for the
shape of the boundary. It validates a typed-job schema and fixture. It has
finite Lean predicates for lifecycle transitions and approval-required jobs.
It has a synthetic plan-execution contract harness that checks active job
receipts and an approval-bypass fixture. It does not have a deployed job
scheduler, permission-enforcement service, approval service, runtime adapter
runner, replay system, Talos runtime, MoECOT runtime reproduction, benchmark,
or security result for this layer.

## Mechanism

A typed job is a small contract for execution.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Plan["Plan node"] --> Job["Typed job"]
  Job --> Lock["Contract lock"]
  Lock --> Perm["Permission check"]
  Perm --> Approve{"Approval required?"}
  Approve -- "yes" --> Gate["Approval gate"]
  Approve -- "no" --> Dispatch["Dispatchable job"]
  Gate --> Dispatch
  Dispatch --> Adapter["Runtime adapter"]
  Adapter --> Work["Work in bounded workspace"]
  Work --> Receipt["Completion receipt"]
  Receipt --> Artifact["Artifact graph"]
  Receipt --> Ledger["Claim / evidence ledger"]
  Receipt --> Residual["Residual or quarantine"]
```

The job record is created from a plan node or required action. It is then
locked against a parent contract so the work cannot quietly change shape
after review. Permission checks decide whether the requested tools, sources,
workspace, data, or runtime effects are allowed. Approval gates block
high-impact work until a scoped approval record exists.

Only after those gates pass does the job become dispatchable.

Dispatch is still not the same as success. The runtime adapter may reject the
job. The workspace may reveal missing inputs. The job may run and produce an
artifact that still needs adjudication. The output may be delivered to a user
while evidence readiness remains incomplete. A responsible Labor OS keeps
those states separate.

This is the key design move: delivery and evidence readiness are different
states. Delivery means output exists or has been returned. Evidence readiness
means required artifact capture, verification, audit events, residuals, and
replay or non-replay fields exist.

## Lifecycle

A useful lifecycle gives the system more than a done/not-done switch.

A drafted job is only a proposed work packet. A locked job has fixed parentage
and expected output. An awaiting-approval job cannot run. An approved job may
still fail a permission or adapter check. A running job must emit audit events.
An adjudicating job has output but not yet acceptance. A delivered job is
useful but not necessarily evidence-ready. A failed or blocked job needs
residuals and recovery. A quarantined job cannot support promotion. A replayed
or retired job becomes historical trace unless it is requalified.

Those distinctions matter because the stack is supposed to learn from its own
work. If every state collapses into done, the system cannot tell whether it
has reusable evidence, a useful draft, an unsafe side effect, or a failure
case.

The lifecycle should reject invalid transitions. A job should not skip from
drafted to running. A high-impact job should not become dispatchable without
approval. A delivered job should not become evidence-ready without the
required receipt fields. A quarantined job should not quietly re-enter the
claim ledger as support.

## Interfaces

The Labor OS sits between planning and artifacts.

Planning supplies the parent plan node. Command contracts supply the authority
and semantic fields. Security supplies permission decisions. Approval supplies
human or policy authorization. Runtime adapters perform bounded effects.
Artifact graphs receive outputs, audit events, replay declarations, and
residuals. Claim ledgers decide whether the result can affect support.

A minimum typed job record should include:

- `job_id`
- `parent_contract_id`
- `plan_node_id`
- `job_type`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `runtime_adapter_ref`
- `input_refs`
- `output_expectation`
- `permission_basis`
- `approval_state`
- `failure_behavior`
- `audit_event_refs`
- `artifact_refs`
- `verification_refs`
- `residuals`
- `replay_status`
- `evidence_state`

The completion receipt is equally important. It should separate request,
approval, permission, adapter invocation, artifact capture, verification,
delivery, residuals, replay or non-replay reason, and evidence state.

That receipt is what prevents completion laundering. A job can be useful
without being evidence-ready. The receipt makes that visible.

## Invariants

Every job has a type and lifecycle state.

Every dispatchable job has a parent contract or plan node.

Tool and runtime permissions are explicit.

Approval-required jobs cannot execute before approval is recorded.

Delivered output is not evidence-ready until verification, artifact capture,
audit, residual, and replay or non-replay fields are present.

Job parentage remains traceable from contract to plan node to approval to
adapter to artifact to residual.

These are execution invariants, not claims of deployed enforcement. The live
repository currently supports them through schema shape, fixtures, finite Lean
predicates, and a synthetic contract harness only.

## Failure Modes

The main failure is unmanaged action.

Tool overreach happens when a job touches a tool, source, credential, network,
filesystem, runtime, or human channel outside its permission basis.

Unlogged side effects happen when output or state changes exist but the job
does not preserve audit events or effect receipts.

Agent swarm drift happens when work is delegated through a cloud of subagents
until no one can tell which job authorized which action.

Completion laundering happens when a generated output is treated as governed
evidence simply because it exists.

Parentage loss happens when a job can no longer be traced back to the command
contract and plan node that made it legitimate.

Approval bypass happens when the system treats predicted approval, implied
approval, or post-hoc approval as equivalent to a scoped approval record.

Each failure should either block dispatch, force quarantine, or leave a
residual that downstream ledgers can see.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a typed-job schema, public fixtures,
finite lifecycle predicates, approval-gate predicates, and a tiny synthetic
job-receipt harness.

The repository currently has the schema and fixture surface. It validates the
typed-job record shape: lifecycle state, approval state, failure behavior,
permissions, audit events, and replay status. `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`
contains finite predicates for declared lifecycle moves and approval-required
jobs. `scripts/validate_plan_execution_contracts.py` checks synthetic active
job receipts and approval-bypass cases.

That is an honest start. It proves that the repository can represent and check
some of the Labor OS boundary. It does not prove that real work is scheduled,
permissioned, approved, adapted, replayed, or secured.

The next honest slice would be a lifecycle harness over fixture jobs: one
valid transition path, one invalid transition, one missing approval, one
missing permission, one delivered-but-not-evidence-ready job, and one
quarantined job. That would strengthen the boundary without pretending a full
labor runtime exists.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is an operating system for bounded cognitive work.

In that endpoint, work does not enter the system as a vague agent intention.
It enters as a typed job whose parent contract, plan node, lifecycle state,
permission basis, approval record, workspace, adapter route, expected
artifact, failure behavior, audit trail, delivery state, residual state, replay
boundary, and evidence state are all explicit.

A mature Labor OS can refuse, approve, dispatch, pause, adjudicate, deliver,
quarantine, replay, or retire jobs through declared transitions. It can show
operators which jobs are waiting on approval, which are running, which are
blocked, which delivered output without evidence readiness, which entered
quarantine, and which artifacts are safe to reuse.

The product-level endpoint is not an agent dashboard. It is an accountable
execution kernel for cognitive labor: typed work packets, permissioned
adapters, scoped approval, isolated workspaces, completion receipts, artifact
graphs, residual ledgers, and replay boundaries.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. The live claim stays at `argument`
until lifecycle engines, permission checks, approval services, adapter
runners, completion receipts, replayable job traces, negative controls,
benchmarks, and security reviews show that cognitive work actually moves
through this governed boundary.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter argues for typed execution discipline. It does not claim that a
Labor OS has been deployed.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, source-note mappings, a public
typed-job schema and fixture, finite Lean predicates, and a synthetic
plan-execution contract harness. Those artifacts support the shape of the
execution boundary. They do not establish scheduler correctness, permission
enforcement, approval-service behavior, adapter safety, runtime isolation,
replay correctness, benchmark performance, security behavior, Talos runtime
behavior, or MoECOT runtime reproduction.

The distinction is central to the chapter. A Labor OS exists to prevent useful
work from being mistaken for governed evidence. The reader draft preserves the
same rule about itself.

## Handoff

Typed jobs make execution bounded. They do not by themselves preserve the full
history of what each job produced, read, changed, failed to prove, or left
behind.

That is the job of Artifact Graphs, Audit Logs, and Replay. Once a job
returns, the stack still needs durable artifact identity, provenance, context
refs, tool refs, audit events, replay grade, claim/test links, residuals, and
evidence gates. The Labor OS hands bounded work to the artifact graph so work
can become inspectable memory instead of disappearing side effect.
